---
title: "8 · Evaluation of the soil state"
---

Every number here is tagged with its **basis** — a genuinely independent test, a shared-forcing
consistency check, or a synthetic demonstration — because the value of a score depends entirely on
which it is ([Evaluation data](02-evaluation-data.qmd)).

## The observing networks, and what each one can say

Four ground networks observe the twin, and they are **not interchangeable**. Each constrains a
different term, and a model can be badly wrong in a way that only one of them can see.

| network | observes | constrains |
|---|---|---|
| **NWIS wells** | depth to water, at a point | the **saturated store** — state, and its seasonal amplitude and phase |
| **USGS gauges** | streamflow, integrated over a basin | the **fluxes** — runoff, interflow, baseflow, via a hydrograph separation |
| **SNOTEL** | SWE and soil moisture, at a point | the **snowpack** — the recharge clock, and the vadose store |
| **Seismic (UW/CC)** | dv/v, over a *volume* between stations | shallow **moisture change**, at storm timescales |

::: {.callout-important}
## Wells alone cannot validate a water budget
The state can be reproduced while the **flux partition** is badly wrong, because nothing in a
depth-to-water record sees where the water went. Streamflow does: it *is* the catchment water budget
integrated over a basin, and a baseflow separation splits it into quickflow and groundwater discharge.
The gauges are therefore not a secondary check on the wells — they constrain a different half of the
problem, and the calibration uses both ([Physics — Hydrology](03a-physics-hydrology.qmd)).
:::

![**The observing networks over the domain, and the spatial support of a dv/v measurement.** Left: the
four ground networks. Centre: the **coda sensitivity** $S(x)$ of the seismic network — the region the
noise cross-correlations actually sample. Right: the measurement uncertainty that follows,
$\sigma \propto S^{-1/2}$, expressed as a multiple of the best-observed cell. dv/v constrains the twin
where the coda samples, and nowhere else.](assets/sensors_and_dvv_support.png){#fig-sensors}

## dv/v: a forward prediction everywhere, a measurement over a volume

::: {.callout-warning}
## The twin's dv/v field is a model output, not an observation
The twin evaluates `dvv_high` and `dvv_low` at **every 90 m cell**. That field is a **forward
prediction**: it is computed from the modelled soil moisture and water table through the petrophysical
coupling ([Physics — Hydromechanics](03b-physics-hydromechanics.qmd)), and it exists wherever the model
exists.

A **measured** dv/v does not live at a cell. It is a property of a **station pair**: the coda of the
noise cross-correlation samples the medium over an extended region, with a sensitivity that is a
spatial kernel rather than a point. Displaying the modelled field everywhere without saying so would
imply the network measures it everywhere.
:::

For diffuse coda, the sensitivity of the travel-time change between receivers $r_1, r_2$ at lapse time
$t$ to a perturbation at $s$ is [@pacheco2005time]

$$
K(s) = \frac{\int_0^t p(r_1, s, u)\, p(s, r_2, t-u)\, \mathrm{d}u}{p(r_1, r_2, t)},
\qquad
p(r,t) = \frac{1}{4\pi D t}\, e^{-r^2/(4Dt)},
$$

with $p$ the intensity Green's function of the diffusion equation. Summing over the available pairs
gives the **network sensitivity** $S(x)=\sum_{\text{pairs}} K_{\text{pair}}(x)$
(`src/models/dvv_sensitivity.py`). Treating pairs as independent observations of the local
perturbation, the uncertainty of a dv/v-derived state scales as

$$\sigma(x) \;\propto\; \big[\,S(x)\,\big]^{-1/2},$$

so a cell with no sensitivity receives **no constraint at all** and falls back on the model — it is
returned as *no constraint*, not as a large-but-finite number that invites interpolation.

This is the **spatial** complement to the *depth* sensitivity of the coda (`codameter.uq_depth`, a
Rayleigh-kernel band matrix). Together they state *where* and *at what depth* a dv/v measurement carries
information. Both limits are real:

- **Depth support.** The shallowest realistic ambient-noise band peaks near ~45 m, below the top-30 m
  Vs30 window, so the Vs30 response to a shallow signal is diluted by the 30 m average — a depth-
  resolution limit, not a tuning artefact.
- **Spatial support.** Sensitivity concentrates near the stations and along the paths between them, and
  broadens with lapse time. Where the network is sparse, a dv/v measurement cannot test the twin.

::: {.callout-note}
## dv/v complements the wells and the soil-moisture sensors; it does not replace them
A well observes the **saturated store** at a point. SNOTEL observes the **vadose store** at a point.
The coda samples a **volume**, and its shallow band responds to moisture on the timescale of individual
storms — which the wells cannot see, because the water table *integrates* storms rather than resolving
them ([Forecast](06-forecast.qmd)).

The three are therefore complementary in **state**, in **support**, and in **timescale**. dv/v is the
right instrument for short-lead, event-scale validation of the moisture state; the wells and gauges are
the right instruments for the cumulative antecedent state and the flux partition.
:::

## Observability: resolution and information gain

The sensitivity map says *where an instrument responds*. The design question is sharper: **how much
does each network actually reduce the uncertainty of the state, and where?** This is a linear-Gaussian
inverse problem (`src/models/observability.py`). The state — a GWL anomaly, or a soil-moisture anomaly
— has a Gaussian prior of correlation length $L$ (the model's own error covariance), and each sensor
is a linear functional of it: a **point** operator for a well or a SNOTEL site, and a **footprint**
operator — the coda kernel — for dv/v. The two states are observed by different instruments:

$$\text{GWL} \;\leftarrow\; \text{wells (point)} + \text{dv/v deep band (volume)},
\qquad
\text{SM} \;\leftarrow\; \text{SNOTEL (point)} + \text{dv/v shallow band (volume)}.$$

From the Gaussian posterior covariance $C_\text{post} = C - CG^\top(GCG^\top + C_d)^{-1}GC$ we read,
per cell, the **resolution** $R = 1 - C_\text{post}/C \in [0,1]$ (the fraction of prior variance the
network removes), the **information gain** $\tfrac12\ln(C/C_\text{post})$ in nats, and the **marginal
gain** of one network beyond another, $R(A\cup B) - R(B)$.

![**Observability of the twin.** Rows: GWL (top), soil moisture (bottom). Columns: the point network
alone; dv/v alone; **the resolution dv/v adds beyond the point network**; and the total information
gain. The point sensors resolve their own neighbourhoods — wells the lowland, SNOTEL a set of isolated
blobs — and leave the space between them unobserved. dv/v, a *volume* measurement, fills that space,
and the third column shows it contributes most **exactly where the point sensors cannot reach**: the
mountain headwaters for GWL, and the whole between-station field for soil
moisture.](assets/observability.png){#fig-observability}

Three results the maps make concrete:

- **Soil moisture is almost unobserved off-station.** SNOTEL is a handful of points; between them the
  prior is unconstrained. dv/v is the *only* network that resolves the soil-moisture field over an
  area, which is the quantitative form of the earlier statement that dv/v *complements* the point
  sensors rather than duplicating them.
- **dv/v extends GWL observation into the ungauged mountains.** Wells sit in the lowland; the marginal-
  gain panel shows dv/v adding resolution precisely in the headwater terrain where wells are absent and
  where the baseline water table is an extrapolation (the applicability-domain caveat below).
- **The design is legible.** Because resolution is a scale-free ratio, these maps depend only on the
  correlation length and the instrument noise-to-prior ratios — not on the absolute prior variance — so
  they read as *where each network is worth its cost*, and where a new station would add the most.

::: {.callout-note}
## What is prior, and what is calibratable
The correlation lengths ($L_\text{GWL} > L_\text{SM}$, since the water table varies more smoothly than
soil moisture) and the per-instrument noise are physical inputs, not fits: the deep dv/v band is a
weaker, noisier handle on the water table than a well, and the shallow band a moderate handle on
moisture. Together with the coda parameters (the sensitivity section above) they should be set from
the network's measured performance; the maps' *pattern* is robust to them, the absolute resolution is
not.
:::

### Satellite and surface-water streams

Two distributed observations enter the same design and change it qualitatively, because they observe
where the ground networks cannot — everywhere, rather than at sites.

- **Satellite soil moisture — a retrieval, not a measurement.** **SMAP** (L-band radiometer, 9 km) and
  the planned **NISAR** (L-band SAR, ~0.2 km) do not *measure* soil moisture; they retrieve it by
  inverting brightness temperature or radar backscatter through a retrieval model, so each is a
  spatially-resolved *estimate* carrying retrieval and vegetation/roughness error. What they uniquely
  provide is **coverage, not accuracy** — a domain-wide constraint where the ground networks are sparse.
  Because the resolution is a scale-free ratio, a not-yet-flown product can be evaluated for the
  coverage it *would* provide.
- **Surface-water extent.** Optical differencing (e.g. Sentinel-2 NDWI) maps inundation, which is the
  **variable source area** — the valley floors and riparian corridors (HAND $\approx 0$) where the
  water table outcrops and quickflow is generated. It is the *spatial* complement to the gauges: a gauge
  measures the basin-integrated discharge, surface-water extent shows *where* that discharge is
  generated, and it pins the shallow water table along the drainage network.

![**The full observing system.** Resolution of the state as each network is added — point (wells,
SNOTEL), volume (dv/v), satellite (SMAP now, NISAR future), and surface water. Top (soil moisture):
SNOTEL is a scatter of points; **SMAP fills the whole domain** at moderate resolution; dv/v adds local
detail; NISAR would approach full resolution. Bottom (groundwater): wells resolve only the lowland;
dv/v reaches the mountains; **surface-water extent lights up the entire drainage network**, the
flux-generating variable source area whose discharge the gauges see only as a basin integral.](assets/observing_system.png){#fig-observing-system}

The observing system is therefore layered by **support**: point sensors anchor specific sites,
satellites blanket the domain at their native resolution, dv/v samples the volume between stations, and
surface water traces the channel network. No single stream is sufficient, and the information-gain
maps show precisely where each one earns its place — which is also the basis for **where a new station,
or a new mission, would add the most**.

### The temporal axis — coverage is not enough for a fast state

Spatial resolution is only half the design. A state that changes fast is observed well only by a stream
that **samples** fast. Soil moisture decorrelates in days — a storm wets the ground and it drains within
a week — so a satellite that revisits weekly **aliases** the very events it exists to capture, however
fine its pixels. The water table integrates months, so a coarse revisit costs it little. The same sensor
is therefore worth different amounts for the two states.

For a state with temporal correlation time $\tau$, a stream sampling at interval $\Delta t$ resolves a
fraction $\exp(-\Delta t / 2\tau)$ of its variability, and a stream's observability of a *dynamic*
field is the product $R_\text{space}\times R_\text{time}$ (`src/models/observability.py`).

![**The observing system on both axes.** Left: each stream in the spatial-support / revisit-interval
plane, with retrievals (satellites) as diamonds and measurements as circles. Streams above the
soil-moisture decorrelation line alias the storm signal. Right: for the *fast* soil-moisture state, the
temporal resolution discounts the coverage — a weekly satellite's fine pixels fall to a fraction of
their nominal worth, while the continuous seismic array and the hourly probes keep theirs. **Great
space is not great time.**](assets/observing_spacetime.png){#fig-spacetime}

This is the quantitative form of "dv/v sees the storm; the water table integrates it": the seismic array
and the sub-daily in-situ probes carry the *temporal* information a satellite cannot, while the satellite
carries the *spatial* coverage they cannot. The design is complementary on **both** axes at once.

## Water-budget evaluation — fluxes and state together

The calibrated budget is scored against both observation classes simultaneously, with each parameter
pinned by a different one ([Physics — Hydrology](03a-physics-hydrology.qmd)):

| | model | observed | basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| baseflow index | 0.52 | 0.47 | **independent** — Lyne–Hollick separation, 6 gauges |
| runoff coefficient $Q/P$ | 0.71 | 0.57–0.73 | **independent** — per-basin closure |
| water-table seasonal amplitude | 1.02 m | 1.06 m | **independent** — 26,816 shallow-well observations |
| water-table peak month | 5 | 4 | **independent** — same wells |
| basin runoff / snow (SWE) | — | ERA5-Land | **cross-check** — independent forcing+model, all three faces at once |

Precipitation is averaged over each gauge's **own watershed** (NLDI polygons). The gauged basins are
Cascade headwater catchments receiving far more orographic precipitation than a domain mean, and
comparing basin discharge against domain-mean rainfall is not a closure. Comparison against wells is
likewise made **at the well locations**: wells are drilled where people live and are not a random sample
of the terrain.

## Groundwater-level evaluation

The baseline is scored by **spatial block cross-validation** with variogram-sized blocks, so nearby
wells never appear in both train and test folds: **block-CV RMSE ≈ 19 m**. A random split would leak,
because lowland wells cannot vouch for mountain cells.

::: {.callout-caution}
## The training wells do not sample the terrain the model must predict
The wells sit in the flat lowland (median slope 3.1°, 1% on ground steeper than 20°); the domain is
37% steep. A tree ensemble asked to predict there does not extrapolate — it returns the nearest
training leaf, so a ridge inherits a lowland water table with a small, confident, and meaningless
variance.

The baseline therefore carries an explicit **applicability domain**: confidence is the distance in
*standardised covariate space* to the nearest training well, not a spatial distance — a cell 200 m from
a well but 30° steeper than anything in training is an extrapolation. It flags **44% of the domain** as
low-confidence, where a physical (TOPMODEL) prior is blended in. The confidence field ships with the
water table; the ensemble spread is documented as a **lower bound** off-sample.
:::

## Soil-moisture evaluation — consistency versus independence

Two comparisons with very different meanings:

- Against **MERRA-2** root-zone reanalysis — a *model evaluation dataset* — the model tracks at
  domain-mean **r = 0.85** (bias −0.08 m³ m⁻³, RMSE 0.095). This is **inter-model agreement**, not
  validation.
- Against **ERA5-Land** — a *different* forcing and land-surface model (HTESSEL/ERA5) — the comparison
  is genuinely more independent than MERRA-2 or TerraClimate, and it uniquely tests soil moisture,
  runoff, and snow on the same grid. Where our snowmelt-driven recharge and ERA5-Land's diverge marks
  a real structural difference worth chasing, not a tuning residual.
- Against **in-situ SNOTEL** θ held out of the estimator — a *genuinely independent* reference — the
  pooled skill is markedly lower in the sparse Cascade uplands, exactly as an honest independent test
  should read. That gap is diagnostic rather than embarrassing: it localised missing physics.

## Snow evaluation

The degree-day module is scored against **30 SNOTEL SWE stations** in the domain (881–1414 m), fitted
on each station's own precipitation and temperature so that the rain/snow-line smearing of a 4 km
gridded product does not enter the calibration. The target is **melt-out timing** rather than SWE RMSE:
RMSE is dominated by the accumulation season, and minimising it selects a melt factor slow enough to
place melt release a month late.

## dv/v recovery — synthetic

The dv/v pipeline is validated end-to-end on a controlled synthetic with a *known* imposed velocity
change: coda-wave stretching recovers the banded $\delta v/v$ at **r > 0.95**, and the depth inversion
returns the imposed profile within its propagated covariance. This is a **synthetic demonstration** —
it establishes that the estimator is unbiased, not that the field measurement is.

## Calibrated uncertainty

Uncertainty is calibrated, not decorative. From out-of-fold $(\mu, \sigma)$ we report empirical
**interval coverage** at the 50/80/90/95% levels, the **PIT** mean, and the **CRPS**. A product whose
stated $\sigma$ does not cover the observations at the nominal rate is recalibrated before release, so
the per-cell $\sigma$ that travels to the hazard models
([Hazard integration](07-hazard-integration.qmd)) means what it says.

Two uncertainties are reported separately and must not be conflated: the **model** uncertainty (the
spread of the estimate) and the **observability** (whether any instrument constrains that cell at all).
The dv/v sensitivity field above is the latter — a cell can have a confident model prediction and no
measurement able to test it.
